mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW Z Series engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned BMW Z Series engine typically costs £2,500–£6,500 supply only [BMW-QUOTE] — from the M54/N52 at £2,500–£5,000 to the N54 at £3,500–£6,500 and the B58 at £3,800–£6,000. The S62 (Z8) costs £5,000–£8,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW Z Series engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58B30 in the G29 M40i — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The M54B30 in the Z3 and early Z4 is the classic gold standard. The N52B30 in the E85/E89 is also excellent.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW Z Series engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but an E89 N54B30 without documented HPFP replacement, injector history, or turbocharger history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A Z3/Z4 M54, N52, or G29 B58 has no such caveat.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my Z Series engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: By engine: long cranking and limp mode (N54 HPFP), coolant loss (M54/N52 cooling system), rattling on cold start (N54 turbo wastegate), VANOS rattle (S54). On the E89 N54, any starting issues are a warning sign. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £2,500–£6,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW Z Series engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends entirely on generation and engine. Against a £15,000–£25,000 G29, a £3,800–£6,000 B58 recon is 15–40% — ✅ safe. Against a £6,000–£10,000 E89, a £3,500–£6,500 N54 recon is 35–108% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ walk away. Against a £5,000–£8,000 E85, a £2,500–£5,000 M54/N52 recon is 31–100% — ⚠️ watch. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW Z Series should I buy?

acceptedAnswer.text: E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 — the sweet spot: naturally-aspirated straight-six, robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values (£5,000–£10,000). For the classic, Z3 3.0i with the M54 — the original modern roadster and a rising classic. For the future, G29 M40i with the B58 — 340 hp, bulletproof, and the last manual Z4. For the collector, Z8 with the S62 — a six-figure classic and a genuine investment. Avoid E89 N54 without documented HPFP history.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW Z Series engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Or tell us your generation and variant (Z3 2.8, Z4 3.0si, Z4 sDrive35i, G29 M40i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: BMW Z4 vs Porsche Boxster — which is more reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Z4 with the N52 or B58 is significantly more reliable than the Boxster's M96/M97 flat-six, which is notorious for IMS bearing failure. The B58 is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines; the N52 is robust and proven. The Boxster offers sharper handling; the Z4 offers better reliability and lower running costs.

hero.h1: Z Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW Z Series engine across five distinct roadster generations — from the M44 four-cylinder of the Z3 to the S62 V8 of the Z8, the N52 straight-six of the E85 Z4, and the B58 straight-six of the G29. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for BMW's iconic roadster family. Backed by real UK Z Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

ownershipVerdict.h2: BMW Z Series — The Ownership Verdict

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The Z Series rule — the roadster premium: a B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G29 is 15–40% — ✅ safe. An N54 recon at £3,500–£6,500 against a £6,000–£10,000 E89 is 35–108% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ scrap. The Z8 is a collector's item where engine work is restoration.
★★★★☆ — the M54, N52 and B58 are the most dependable; the N54 carries HPFP and injector caveats; the S62 is robust but ageing; the S54 is a masterpiece with VANOS caveats
B58B30 (G29 M40i) — widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines • M54B30 (Z3/Z4) — the classic straight-six gold standard
N54B30 (E89 sDrive35i) — HPFP failure, injector issues, turbo wastegate rattle
E85 Z4 3.0si with N52 — the naturally-aspirated straight-six is robust, the chassis is magnificent, and values are still sensible
G29 Z4 M40i with B58 — 340 hp, the most dependable modern roadster engine, and the last manual Z4
N54 HPFP / cooling system — shared-platform data [BMW-VERIFIED]
🏆 The Roadster Icon — BMW's most celebrated two-seater sports car lineage

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The Z Series is BMW's roadster icon — five generations of open-top driving pleasure. The M54 and N52 are the robust classics; the N54 is the high-risk turbo; the B58 is the modern gold standard. Buy the straight-six — the four-cylinder Z3s are for collectors, the Z8 is for investors, and the G29 is for drivers who want the best of both worlds.

atAGlance.h2: BMW Z Series at a Glance

rows.value: H2: BMW Z Series Generations — Find Your Vehicle

rows.metric: Sub-headline: Five generations, 30 years, and one mission — to deliver the ultimate open-top driving experience. The right Z Series depends on which generation and which engine you choose.

rows.value: 1995–2002 • M44, M52, M54, S54 — E36/E46 platform-based

Our Verdict: The original modern BMW roadster — the car that launched the Z Series and defined 1990s open-top driving. The M52 and M54 straight-sixes are the robust picks; the S54 in the Z3 M is a masterpiece with VANOS caveats; the M44 four-cylinder is the entry point. Buy the six-cylinder — the four-cylinder is for collectors.

Our Verdict: The ultimate BMW roadster — a limited-production V8 masterpiece designed by Henrik Fisker. The S62 V8 is shared with the E39 M5, producing 394 bhp. Now a six-figure classic where engine work is restoration, not repair. The Z8 is the Z Series for investors and collectors.

rows.metric: BMW Z4 E85/E86 🔥 Most Enquired — N52 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The first-generation Z4 — Chris Bangle's controversial styling that aged beautifully. The M54 and N52 straight-sixes are the robust picks; the S54 in the Z4 M is a masterpiece with VANOS caveats. The E85 roadster is the classic open-top experience; the E86 coupé is the rare driver's choice.

rows.metric: BMW Z4 E89 🔥 Most Enquired — N54/N55 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The retractable hardtop Z4 — the most technologically advanced Z Series. The N52 straight-six is the robust pick; the N54 twin-turbo carries HPFP and injector caveats; the N55 is the improved turbo. Buy the N52 or N55 — the N54 is the trap without documented history.

rows.metric: BMW Z4 G29 🔥 Most Enquired — B58 drives volume [BMW-VERIFIED]

rows.value: Our Verdict: The second-generation Z4 — CLAR platform, B-family engines, and the most dependable Z Series ever made. The B58 in the M40i produces 340 hp and is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines. The G29 is the Z Series for the modern era — buy it with confidence.

rows.metric: The Badge Ambiguity — "Z4" Means Different Things
rows.value: Correction applied (internal): The "Z4" badge covers three different generations with different engines:

rows.metric: * E85/E86 (2002–2008): M54, N52, S54 — the classic roadster/coupé
rows.value: * E89 (2009–2016): N52, N54, N55 — the retractable hardtop

rows.metric: * G29 (2019–present): B48, B58 — the modern roadster
rows.value: The "Z3" badge covers the original roadster (1995–2002) with M44, M52, M54, and S54 engines. The Z8 is a separate model entirely with the S62 V8. All share a badge family. None share a reliability profile across generations.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW Z Series Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW Z Series — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: B58B30, N52B30 and M54B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume engines with deep specialist rebuild history and clear failure patterns from the wider BMW range. N54B30 rating: 85% confidence — HPFP and injector issues are extensively documented. S54B32 and S62B50 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented by M car and specialist consensus. Z3 four-cylinder engines: Pattern-data tier — documented by enthusiast records. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The Z3 (1995–2002) used M44 (1.9L four-cylinder), M52 (2.0L, 2.8L six-cylinder), M54 (2.2L, 3.0L six-cylinder), and S54 (3.2L six-cylinder in the Z3 M). The M52 and M54 are the robust picks. Correction applied: The Z8 (1999–2003) uses the S62 V8 — the same engine as the E39 M5. It produces 394 bhp and is one of BMW's most revered engines. Correction applied: The Z4 E85 (2002–2008) used M54 (2002–2005) and N52 (2006–2008) six-cylinder engines, plus the S54 in the Z4 M. The N52 replaced the M54 in 2006. Correction applied: The Z4 E89 (2009–2016) used N52 (sDrive23i/30i), N54 (sDrive35i, 2009–2011), and N55 (sDrive35i, 2011–2016). The N54 carries HPFP and injector caveats; the N55 is the improved version. Correction applied: The Z4 G29 (2019–present) uses B48 (sDrive20i/30i) and B58 (M40i) engines. The B58 produces 340 hp and is widely regarded as one of BMW's most reliable modern engines.

commonProblems.h2: Common BMW Z Series Engine Problems
commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across the Z Series range — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining E89 Z4 issue — high-pressure fuel pump failure causes long cranking and limp mode; injector issues follow. Affects E89 sDrive35i (2009–2011). BMW extended warranty covered many; check history before buying. £3,500–£6,500 recon.

The M54's plastic cooling system components degrade — water pump impellers, expansion tanks, and thermostat housings fail. Overheating kills the alloy head. Affects Z3 3.0i and E85 3.0i. Replace proactively every 60k miles.
The N52's Valvetronic eccentric shaft sensor can fail; the cooling system (water pump, thermostat) is also a weak point. Affects E85 3.0si and E89 sDrive23i/30i. £500–£1,500 repair.

The S54's VANOS pump can fail, causing loss of power; rod bearing wear is also documented. Affects Z3 M and Z4 M (2001–2008). £5,000–£8,000 recon. Preventative VANOS: £1,500–£3,000; rod bearings: £2,500–£4,000.
Not an engine failure — but the most common non-engine Z3 enquiry. The rear subframe mounting points can crack on high-mileage Z3s. Check on any Z3 over 80k miles.
The B58's known weak point — coolant expansion tank leaks and water pump failures. Affects G29 M40i (2019+). £400–£800 repair, not catastrophic if caught early.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW Z Series Market Intelligence — What UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising — the G29 is entering used market [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
➡ Stable — enthusiast market, not failure-driven [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]
⬇ Falling — the N54 is being phased out

insights: Interesting insight: The Z Series generates a different kind of enquiry than the rest of the BMW range — owners are enthusiasts doing due diligence on specific known issues (N54 HPFP, M54 cooling, S54 VANOS) rather than families facing unexpected failures. The Z Series buyer is often a weekend driver making an emotional purchase, making the engine choice a critical decision point. The N52-powered E85 is the sweet spot — robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

The Z3 is now a classic — £4,000–£12,000 for a used example. The M54 3.0i is the one to buy; the S54 Z3 M is the collector's piece. The Z8 is a six-figure classic — engine work on a Z8 is restoration, not repair. (Editorial synthesis.)

The Z4 E85 with the N52 is the sweet spot in the used market — £5,000–£10,000 for a well-maintained example. The N52 is robust, the styling has aged beautifully, and the driving experience is pure BMW. The E89 with the N54 is the one to avoid without documented HPFP history. The G29 with the B58 is the future — the B58 is bulletproof, and the G29 is the last manual Z4. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW Z Series Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one Z Series based on this page, buy an E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 — it's the sweet spot in the range: the naturally-aspirated straight-six is robust, the chassis is magnificent, and the values are still sensible (£5,000–£10,000). If your budget stretches, the G29 M40i with the B58 is the most dependable modern roadster BMW has ever made. And on any E89 N54, budget for HPFP history before you buy — it's £3,500–£6,500 well spent.

replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW Z Series engine typically takes 10–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The Z Series rule — the roadster premium: A B58 recon at £3,800–£6,000 against a £15,000–£25,000 G29 sits at 15–40% — ✅ safe. An N54 recon at £3,500–£6,500 against a £6,000–£10,000 E89 is 35–108% — ⚠️ watch to ❌ walk away. An M54/N52 recon at £2,500–£5,000 against a £5,000–£8,000 E85 is 31–100% — ⚠️ watch on the cheapest cars. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. The E85 N52 is the sweet spot — robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values. The G29 B58 is the safe modern choice. The E89 N54 is the one to avoid without HPFP history.

The E85 N52 is the sweet spot — robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values. The G29 B58 is the safe modern choice. The E89 N54 is the one to avoid without HPFP history.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW Z Series Engine Range Evolved — 1995 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW launched the Z3 as the modern roadster — the M44 four-cylinder was the entry point; the M52 and M54 straight-sixes were the driver's choice; the S54 in the Z3 M was the ultimate.
eras.worthKnowing: The Z3 is the original modern BMW roadster — the M52 and M54 are the robust picks; the S54 is a masterpiece with VANOS caveats. The Z3 M is a collector's item.

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW created the Z8 as a limited-production V8 roadster inspired by the 507. The S62 V8 was shared with the E39 M5, producing 394 bhp.
eras.worthKnowing: The Z8 is a six-figure classic — engine work is restoration, not repair. The S62 V8 is one of BMW's most revered engines.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The Z4 replaced the Z3 with more aggressive styling and improved dynamics. The M54 was used initially; the N52 replaced it in 2006. The S54 powered the Z4 M.
eras.worthKnowing: The E85 Z4 is the classic modern BMW roadster — the N52 is the robust pick. The Z4 M with the S54 is the collector's choice. The E86 coupé is the rare driver's choice.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The E89 Z4 introduced a retractable hardtop, the N52 straight-six, and the N54 twin-turbo. The N55 replaced the N54 in 2011.
eras.worthKnowing: The N54 carries HPFP and injector caveats — the N52 and N55 are the safer choices. The E89 is the most technologically advanced Z4.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The G29 Z4 returned to a fabric roof and the CLAR platform with B-family engines. The B58 M40i produces 340 hp and is one of BMW's most reliable modern engines.
eras.worthKnowing: The G29 is the Z Series for the modern era — the B58 is bulletproof, and the G29 is the last manual Z4. Buy it with confidence.

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW Z Series?

profiles.verdict: The Z Series is built for this — the E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 is the sweet spot: robust engine, classic styling, and sensible values (£5,000–£10,000). The G29 M40i is the modern alternative — 340 hp, B58 bulletproof reliability, and the last manual Z4.

The Z3 M with the S54 is a rising classic — the ultimate Z3 and a future investment. The Z8 is the ultimate BMW roadster — a six-figure classic and a genuine investment. The Z4 M with the S54 is the modern classic — the E86 coupé is the rarest and most desirable.

The Z Series is a weekend car, not a daily driver — the Z4 E89 with the N52 is the most practical, but the Z3 and Z4 E85 are cramped and loud. The G29 is more refined but still a two-seater. Buy the Z Series for the weekend — buy a 3 Series for the daily.

A cheap E89 N54 is not a cheap Z4 — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £6,000 E85 3.0si with N52 is the bargain; a £5,000 E89 N54 without HPFP history is the trap. The Z3 2.8 with M52 is the classic budget choice.

The E85 Z4 3.0si with the N52 is the modern keeper — the N52 is robust, the chassis is magnificent, and the values are only going one way. The G29 M40i with the B58 is the future keeper — the B58 will outlast the car around it, and the manual gearbox makes it a future classic.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My BMW Z Series Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Cold-start rattle on an N54? Cooling system failure on an M54? On the Z Series, the answer depends on which generation and which engine you have — an E89 N54 and a G29 B58 face very different economics. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.
